20 Appendix E: iDRIVE Scan Systems – Control Commands and Signals Transmitted to RTC Boards 
1204
innovators for industry
Comments (RTC6) 
• The scan system firmware a.bb.c is read as abbc, 
for example, 5100 corresponds to version 5.10.0, 
and 5054 to version 5.05.4. 
• On SetMode, SetControlDefinitionMode and 
SetEchoMode 
– The data type selected by 
control_command(CodeHIGH = 05H, 0EH or 21H 
or Data = 1700H) is transmitted until another 
data type is selected.
– Data transmitted from the scan system to the 
RTC board can be queried by: 
• get_value 
• get_values 
• set_trigger[*] 
• get_waveform 
The first data after switching the data source 
are transmitted only after a short time delay 
due to the serial transmission times. Therefore, 
a delay time of up to 60 s may occur between 
the switchover time and the first output of the 
new data type. control_command always 
automatically inserts a waiting time of 60 µs 
after the data source is switched (the previously 
mentioned commands always return correct 
values). 
– All data returned from the scan system to the 
RTC6 are passed over as signed 20-bit values. 
This applies even if the RTC6 DLL is set to 
RTC4 Compatibility Mode and even for 
scan systems without SL2-100 interface, 
controlled by an XY2-100 Converter 
(Accessory). Queried data returned by 
get_value, get_values or get_waveform are 
nevertheless generally transferred to the PC as 
signed 32-bit values (for data evaluation, see 
comments for get_value).
– For scan systems with integrated SL2-100 
interface, get_head_status queries the 
XY2-100 status word regardless of settings 
made by control_command(CodeHIGH = 05H, 0EH 
or 21H or Data = 1700H). It is returned in 
addition to the selected data. In contrast, if 
iDRIVE Scan Systems (without an integrated 
SL2-100 interface) are controlled by an 
XY2-100 Converter (Accessory), 
get_head_status returns the 
XY2-100 status word only if this signal has 
been previously selected to be returned from 
the scan system by control_command, see 
also Section ”Reading Out Data”, Page 233. 
– After a reset or power-up of the scan system, it 
can take around 5 seconds for data to be 
returned from the scan system (see also 
get_value). After a reset or power-up of the 
scan system, always the XY2-100 status word is 
returned. 
• On SetMode 
– Set position (angular position) values returned 
by the scan system correspond to the effective 
output values Sample<AX…BY>_Out with 
set_trigger[*]. Additionally, the 20-bit 
set position values returned from a z axis in 
RTC5 Compatibility Mode (and in 
RTC4 Compatibility Mode from the x axis and 
y axis, too) are scaled by a factor 16 relating to 
the therefore specified 16-bit coordinate 
values.
– The angle bit-values (actual position, 
set position, position error) or angle bit/ms-
values (actual speed) returned to the RTC6 can 
be converted into °-values or °/ms-values by the 
scan system’s calibration angle. The calibration 
angle can be read out by Data = 0523H.
– Exact values for the internal voltages referred to 
in the table can vary for different versions of 
the scan system.
– intelliWELD scan systems can be supplied with 
various number of sensors. Therefore, observe 
the manual of the respective scan system. This 
manual lists the command codes for currently 
available sensors.

[p.1205]
RTC6 Manual 
Doc. Rev. 1.1.3 en-US 
20 Appendix E: iDRIVE Scan Systems – Control Commands and Signals Transmitted to RTC Boards 
1205
innovators for industry
• On SetPositionScale 
– If the scaling factor for scaling the position 
values is changed, then the user program 
calibration factor for calculating RTC6 control 
values (in bits) from the desired Image Field 
position values (in mm), see Chapter 7.3.2 
”Image Field Size and Image Field Calibration”, 
Page 180, needs to be subjected to an inverse 
proportional scaling. In addition, the 
jump speed and mark speed should be 
adjusted.
– The currently set scaling factor can be queried 
with Data = 053FH. 
• On SetPosAcknowledgeLevel 
– The limit value must be specified related to a 
16-bit position range. 
– The default start behavior is for the scan system 
to set the limit value to 0.28% of the full 
angular range of an axis (LSB16 or 1/65536, 
CodeLOW = 183 = B7H) after every power-up 
or reset. 
If other limit values are desired, they must be 
separately set for each axis (CodeHIGH = 15H). 
SCANLAB recommends setting only limit values 
(CodeLOW) > 14H (that is, 0.03% of the full 
position range). Lower values can lead to 
frequent system safety shutdowns due to 
Position Acknowledge time outs (set position 
not reached for an excessive time). 
• On Store/RestoreTransmissionMode 
– control_command( Data = 1700H ) has no effect 
if a power-up or reset was executed after the 
most recent execution of control_command( Data 
= 17FFH ).

[p.1206]
RTC6 Manual 
Doc. Rev. 1.1.3 en-US 
21 Appendix F: About Return Channel Multiplexing and Extended Scan Head Status 
1206
innovators for industry
21 Appendix F: About Return Channel Multiplexing and 
Extended Scan Head Status
Non-Return Channel Multiplexing-capable 
iDRIVE scan systems can only transport 1 signal type 
on the Return Channel per 10 µs clock cycle, that is, 
for example: 
• Either galvanometer scanner position 
• Or an “other” signal(1) 
More possibilities for signal transmission on the 
Return Channel have been opened up by the 
introduction of time division multiplexing. 
iDRIVE scan systems which support these, now can 
simultaneously transmit several signal types per 
Return Channel Multiplexing block(2)(b)in a common 
transmission channel (here: the Return Channel).(3) 
The criterion for differentiating between the signal 
transmission options is the temporal resolution. 
• Signals with a high update rate, 
for example, positions. 
These are required in the highest possible 
temporal resolution (= each block(2)). 
For this purpose, Return Channel Multiplexing, 
Page 1208 (RCM) is available. 
• Signals with a low refresh rate, 
for example, serial number. 
These are only required in a low temporal 
resolution (= not each block(2)). 
They are made available by 
Extended Scan Head Status, Page 1214. 
(1) From a user’s perspective – would even be retrieved in 
clock cycle times that are unusable. 
(2) Not: “SL2-100 block”, see SL2-100 Protocol Short 
Information, Page 1207. 
(3) However, this simultaneous transmission of multiple 
signals comes at the expense of lower temporal 
resolution.

[p.1207]
RTC6 Manual 
Doc. Rev. 1.1.3 en-US 
21 Appendix F: About Return Channel Multiplexing and Extended Scan Head Status 
1207
innovators for industry
SL2-100 Protocol Short Information 
SL2-100 protocol: 
• Is bidirectional 
• Is serial 
• Requires 2 separate channels 
– Forward Channel(a) 
(designated here from the perspective of the RTC board) 
• 1 channel for the direction RTC board →iDRIVE scan system 
• In Figure 15: cable wire from pin 05 →pin 01 (blue) and cable wire from pin 09 →pin 06 
– Return Channel 
(designated here from the perspective of the RTC board) 
• 1 channel for the direction iDRIVE scan system →RTC board 
• In Figure 15: cable wire from pin 05 →pin 01 (red) and cable wire from pin 09 →pin 06 
• Data in Forward Channel and Return Channel are transported in blocks 
(“SL2-100 block”, “transmission block”) 
• With SL2-100 protocol, Forward Channel and the Return Channel are completely independent of each 
other 
• Accordingly, both have their own SL2-100 blocks 
(= SL2-100 Forward Channel block and SL2-100 Return Channel block) 
• A block start in one direction does not automatically mean a block start in the other direction(b) 
• The following applies: 
– 1 block consists of 192 frames = 
 
– Each block starts with a preamble (generated by the RTC board) 
– 1 frame consists of 2 sub-frames 
– 1 frame is transmitted in 10 µs 
– 1 sub-frame contains 
• 20 bits of payload 
(control values or status values) 
• 12 bits of further information 
(for example, for synchronization) 
(a) Synonym: outward channel. 
(b) Therefore, with Return Channel Multiplexing, synchronization is to Return Channel block start only. Return Channel 
Multiplexing is independent of the Forward Channel itself. Furthermore, maximum Return Channel 
Multiplexing block length is 16. 
“block length”

[p.1208]
RTC6 Manual 
Doc. Rev. 1.1.3 en-US 
21 Appendix F: About Return Channel Multiplexing and Extended Scan Head Status 
1208
innovators for industry
21.1 Return Channel Multiplexing
Prerequisites 
• Transmitter-side 
SCANLAB iDRIVE scan system 
– Must support the SL2-100 protocol natively, 
that is, do not use a converter! 
– Must have installed scan system firmware 
which supports the following:(1) 
• control_command( Data = 65nnH ) 
• control_command( Data = 66nnH ) 
• control_command( Data = 0574H ) 
• Receiver-side 
RTC6 board (also: RTC6 Ethernet Board) 
RTC6 Software Package ≥1.19.1 
With Return Channel Multiplexing, the 
iDRIVE scan system (= transmitter) now transmits 
several signals one after the other in time (= time 
multiplexing) in the Return Channel block. For the 
purpose of differentiation, in the following, this 
modified SL2-100 block is referred to as the “Return 
Channel Multiplexing”. 
Return Channel Multiplexing is designed for signals 
that change frequently (= high update rate). For 
these, the highest possible (= each block(2)) temporal 
resolution is demanded, for example, 
• set position 
• actual position 
• actual speed 
The following applies: 
• Each signal is transmitted with the full resolution 
of 20 bits per 10 µs clock cycle 
• With n signals, the temporal resolution thus is 
100 kHz / n 
With Return Channel Multiplexing, you can have the 
same signals transmitted as by SetMode. The 
possible CodeLOW values (and its meanings) are thus 
identical. 
Basic Procedure 
(1) By corresponding control_command calls(3), you 
define: 
– The signals to be transmitted 
• their sequence 
• their respective “block length” 
In order for the data to be synchronizable (by 
means of preamble(2)) from the Forward Channel 
block and the Return Channel block, you must set 
the Return Channel Multiplexing “block length”. 
Allowed values: 1; 2; 3; 4; 6; 8; 12; 16 (= integer 
divisor of 192 ≥16). See Chapter 21.1.1 
”Configuring which Values are to be Transmitted 
(Example Code)”, Page 1209 
(2) Triggering Transmission 
(3) Recording with RTC board by set_trigger[*] call 
– dedicated signal type (63, 64, 65, 66) 
(4) Read out by get_waveform or Data Streaming. 
(5) Assigning Values (Demultiplexing). 
You must implement demultiplexing yourself. 
To do this, you must know, among other things, 
which configuration (“block length”, signals 
including sequence) is set in step 1. 
Notes 
• Return Channel Multiplexing and 
“Spot Distance Control” are not compatible. 
(1) Otherwise, Return Channel Multiplexing behaves 
undefined. 
(2) See SL2-100 Protocol Short Information, Page 1207. 
(3) control_command always applies only to 1 axis, see 
function signature control_command( Head, Axis, Data ).

[p.1209]
RTC6 Manual 
Doc. Rev. 1.1.3 en-US 
21 Appendix F: About Return Channel Multiplexing and Extended Scan Head Status 
1209
innovators for industry
21.1.1 Configuring which Values are 
to be Transmitted 
(Example Code)
The following example code uses control_command 
calls to set iDRIVE scan system 1 to transmit x axis 
signal values using Return Channel Multiplexing. 
// Pseudo code, not executable
// For x axis of iDRIVE scan system 1
// put out 4 signal values. 
// Return Channel Multiplexing-“block length” 
// must be an an integer divisor of 192 ≤16: 
// 1; 2; 3; 4; 6; 8; 12; 16. 
// Return Channel Multiplexing-
// “block length” = 4
control_command( 1, 1, 0x6503 );
// Configuring Signal 0 = to configure Index 0 
// At which point the 1st data type is transferred 
control_command( 1, 1, 0x6580 );
// Signal 0 = 0x01 (actual position)
control_command( 1, 1, 0x6601 );
// Configuring Signal 1 = to configure Index 1 
// At which point the 2nd data type is transferred
control_command( 1, 1, 0x6581 );
// Signal 1 = 0x02 (set position)
control_command( 1, 1, 0x6602 );
// Configuring Signal 2 = to configure Index 2
control_command( 1, 1, 0x6582 );
// Signal 2 = 0x06 (actual speed)
control_command( 1, 1, 0x6606 );
// Configuring Signal 3 = to configure Index 3
control_command( 1, 1, 0x6583 ); 
// Signal 3 = 0x04 (actual stage current)
control_command( 1, 1, 0x6604 );
// Starting transmission by iDRIVE scan system 
control_command( 1, 1, 0x0574 ); 
// Recording by RTC board as usual
// however, with dedicated signal type (63, 64, 65, 66).
// set_trigger/set_trigger4/set_trigger8 call 
// Downloading to PC (reafing out) as usual
// get_waveform call
// Demultiplexing section, see Chapter 21.1.3 ”Assigning Values (Demultiplexing)”, Page 1212

[p.1210]
RTC6 Manual 
Doc. Rev. 1.1.3 en-US 
21 Appendix F: About Return Channel Multiplexing and Extended Scan Head Status 
1210
innovators for industry
The following table shows an example of a section of 
a Return Channel Multiplexing data recording. 
• Column WaveformIndex makes clear that it is a 
section of a larger amount of recorded data (thus, 
for example, some hundred thousand data 
points). The entries at position 123456…123461 are 
examined. 
• Column Forward Channel Block shows the 
imaginary frame counter of the SL2-100 Return 
Channel. This is set back to 0 at the beginning of 
each block. 
• Column Return Channel Block contains the 
imaginary index of the Return Channel 
Multiplexing Return Channel. At the SL2-100 
block start (column Forward Channel Block = 0) it 
is 0, then it counts up to the highest index (N with 
control_command( Data = 650NH )), and is then set 
back to 0. 
• Column Data contains the actual read data. 
• Column Signal names which data it is.

21 Appendix F: About Return Channel Multiplexing and Extended Scan Head Status 
1211
innovators for industry
21.1.2 Triggering Transmission
• To start the transmission of the signals by the 
iDRIVE scan system, call SetMode with channel 
number 0x74: 
control_command( 1, 1, 0x0574 ); (1) 
After that, the iDRIVE scan system transmits the 
configured values in Round-robin scheduling (see 
Wikipedia article). 
Notes 
• The value for the current 10 µs clock cycle is 
always transferred. If you commanded to 
transmit actual position and set position 
alternately, the values come from different 
10 µs clock cycles. Therefore, you can not 
conclude the position error here! 
• The Return Channel Multiplexing block length 
value must be: 1; 2; 3; 4; 6; 8; 12; 16 (= integer 
divisor of 192 ≤16). 
Block start of Forward Channel as well as of 
Return Channel occur simultaneously. “block 
length” for the SL2-100 protocol is 192. 
Accordingly, the Return Channel Multiplexing 
block is cut off, if its length is not divisible by 192 
(potential source of error!). 
(1) control_command always applies only to 1 axis, see 
function signature control_command( Head, Axis, Data ).

[p.1212]
RTC6 Manual 
Doc. Rev. 1.1.3 en-US 
21 Appendix F: About Return Channel Multiplexing and Extended Scan Head Status 
1212
innovators for industry
21.1.3 Assigning Values 
(Demultiplexing)
To be able to assign the values correctly again (based 
on the block starts), set_trigger provides dedicated 
signal types for recording Return Channel 
Multiplexing values (as of RTC6 Software Package 
≥1.19.1): 
• Signal type 63 
= StatusAX_Synced 
• Signal type 64 
= StatusAY_Synced 
• Signal type 65 
= StatusBX_Synced 
• Signal type 66 
= StatusBY_Synced 
After the corresponding set_trigger[*] call the 
requested signal value can be found in 
Bit #19…Bit #0 (of a sub-frame). 
The begin of a new SL2-100 block start is signaled 
once every 192 clock cycles by Bit #20 = 1. 
This allows you to assign the values to the previously 
set indices/data types of the Return Channel block. 
You can assign the values yourself with a user-defined 
function (“unravel the series of numbers”), or use 
instead: 
• demux_rcm_ctrl, 
see Example Code (C), Page 1213 
If you only need a single value, use: 
• get_rcm_value_ctrl, 
see Example Code (C), Page 1213

[p.1213]
RTC6 Manual 
Doc. Rev. 1.1.3 en-US 
21 Appendix F: About Return Channel Multiplexing and Extended Scan Head Status 
1213
innovators for industry
Example Code (C)
// Pseudo code, not executable
// It is assumed that the scan system is configured according to:
// Chapter 21.1.1 ”Configuring which Values are to be Transmitted (Example Code)”, Page 1209
// Create list for data recording
set_start_list_1();
set_trigger(1, 63, 0);
long_delay(10000);
set_end_of_list();
// Execute list and wait for end
execute_list_1();
UINT status, pos;
do {
get_status(&status, &pos);
} while (status != 0);
// Read out data
UINT rcm_data[10000];
get_waveform(1, 10000, rcm_data);
// Demultiplex
UINT actual_positions[2500];
UINT n_actual_positions = demux_rcm_ctrl(rcm_data, 10000, 4, 0, 0, actual_positions);
// Read out single value
UINT set_position = get_rcm_value_ctrl(1, 1, 1);

[p.1214]
RTC6 Manual 
Doc. Rev. 1.1.3 en-US 
21 Appendix F: About Return Channel Multiplexing and Extended Scan Head Status 
1214
innovators for industry
21.2 Extended Scan Head Status
Prerequisites 
• Transmitter-side 
SCANLAB iDRIVE scan system 
– scan system firmware ≥51*7 
– scan system firmware < 5900 
(Version number of scan system firmware is 
transmitted after: 
control_command( 0x0522 ); ) 
• Receiver-side 
RTC6 Software Package ≥1.19.1 
Signals that change relatively rare or not at all (= low 
update rate), for example, are: 
• Temperatures 
• Voltages 
• Serial number 
• Firmware 
• Configuration of Return Channel Multiplexing 
For such signals, a lower (= not every block(2)) 
temporal resolution is sufficient. They are transmitted 
by means of Extended Scan Head Status. For the 
complete signal list, see Chapter 21.2.1 
”Extended Scan Head Status Signal IDs”, Page 1215. 
The transmission of these signals by the 
iDRIVE scan system is active by default (see 
Prerequisites). No action is required on the user side 
for this. 
For Extended Scan Head Status, a previously unused 
bit of the SL2-100 protocol is used. Therefore, the 
following applies: 
• A signal (from Chapter 21.2.1 
”Extended Scan Head Status Signal IDs”, 
Page 1215) requires 320 µs transmission time 
(= 1 bit per 10 µs clock cycle). 
Accordingly, 46 (256) different signals would be 
updated every 14.72 ms (81.92 ms). 
Available to query Extended Scan Head Status are, 
see Prerequisites: 
• Control command 
get_extended_head_status_value_ctrl 
Returns only a single 
Extended Scan Head Status signal value of an 
axis. 
Example code: 
UINT aperture = 
get_extended_head_status_value_ctrl( 1, 1, 0x0
D ); 
UINT wave_length = 
get_extended_head_status_value_ctrl( 1, 1, 0x0
E ); 
• Control command 
get_extended_head_status_block_ctrl 
Writes all possible Extended Scan Head Status 
signal values of an axis into the array Ptr. 
Example code: 
UINT Buffer[256]; 
get_extended_head_status_block_ctrl( 1, 1, (ULONG
_PTR) Ptr ); 
UINT Aperture = Ptr[0x0D]; 
UINT Wavelength = Ptr[0x0E]; 
Notes
• Extended Scan Head Status and Data Streaming 
are not compatible. 
• Thus, Extended Scan Head Status values cannot 
be recorded by set_trigger[*] with the RTC board 
and then downloaded (read out) to the PC by 
get_waveform. 
Instead, a part of the corresponding data is sent 
along in the meta data of each Data Streaming 
data packet.

[p.1215]
RTC6 Manual 
Doc. Rev. 1.1.3 en-US

22 Appendix G: About Calibration Factors
• Source: “Calibrating a 3-Axis Laser Scan System” 
Manual Doc. Rev. 1.5.0 en-US 
Whether a calibration factor specification is to be 
interpreted as a 16-bit value or a 20-bit value 
depends on the ReadMe file from which it is taken, 
see Table 12, Page 1222. 
In principle, only use the calibration factor 
specifications that SCANLAB has been specified for 
the corresponding correction file. 
Notes 
•
The calibration factor is stored as a 20-bit value in 
the file header of a ct5-correction file 
(a ctb-correction file does not have a file header). 
•
See also Chapter 7.3.2 ”Image Field Size and 
Image Field Calibration”, Page 180.
